title: Lyme Borreliosis
authors: Allen C. Steere, Jacob E. Lemieux

items.title: Understanding the Causative Agent and Transmission of Lyme Disease
items.content: Lyme borreliosis is caused by spirochetes from the Borrelia burgdorferi sensu lato complex, transmitted via Ixodes ticks. The disease progresses through three stages: localized infection with erythema migrans (EM), disseminated infection affecting multiple systems, and persistent infection leading to chronic symptoms.
keyPoints: Lyme disease is caused by Borrelia burgdorferi sensu lato transmitted by Ixodes ticks.
The spirochete's genome has a linear chromosome (~950 kb) and 17–21 plasmids, encoding over 100 lipoproteins.
Four main genospecies cause Lyme disease: B. burgdorferi (US), B. garinii, B. bavariensis, and B. afzelii (Europe/Asia).
WGS type A strains are particularly virulent due to a large pangenome and immunogenic lipoproteins.
items.reference: Lyme Borreliosis · Lyme Borreliosis | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.title: Genomic Characteristics of B. burgdorferi and Their Implications
items.content: B. burgdorferi's genome is unique with a small size (~1.5 Mb) and includes a linear chromosome and multiple plasmids, encoding numerous lipoproteins that interact with hosts or vectors.
keyPoints: The B. burgdorferi genome consists of a 950 kb linear chromosome and 17–21 plasmids.
Over 100 known or predicted lipoproteins are encoded on these plasmids, contributing to pathogenicity.
B. burgdorferi depends heavily on its host for nutritional needs due to limited biosynthetic proteins.
items.reference: Lyme Borreliosis · Lyme Borreliosis | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.title: Strain Variability and Pathogenicity in Lyme Disease
items.content: Different strains of B. burgdorferi exhibit varying pathogenicity, with WGS type A being particularly virulent due to its extensive pangenome and numerous immunogenic lipoproteins.
keyPoints: B. burgdorferi strains are classified by typing schemes such as OspC variation, 16S–23S rRNA intergenic spacer region differences, multilocus sequence typing, and whole genome sequencing (WGS).
WGS type A strains have a large pangenome and numerous plasmid-encoded lipoproteins, increasing their virulence.
These strains are implicated in the epidemic emergence of Lyme disease in the northeastern United States.
items.reference: Lyme Borreliosis · Lyme Borreliosis | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.title: Understanding Lyme Disease Transmission Dynamics
items.content: Lyme disease transmission is primarily facilitated by Ixodes ticks, with I. scapularis being the main vector in the eastern and midwestern United States, while I. pacificus serves this role in the western states. The lifecycle of these ticks involves multiple stages requiring blood meals, with nymphal ticks posing the highest risk for human transmission during early summer.
keyPoints: Ixodes scapularis is the principal vector in eastern and midwestern U.S.; Ixodes pacificus in the west.
Transmission peaks in early summer due to nymphal tick activity.
Life cycle involves larval, nymphal, and adult stages with specific host preferences; white-footed mice are crucial for immature ticks.
Lyme disease is the most common vector-borne infection in the U.S. and Europe.
>30,000 new cases reported each summer in the U.S., but true annual incidence estimated at 476,000.

items.title: Geographic Distribution and Vector Diversity of Lyme Disease
items.content: Lyme disease is prevalent across various regions due to different Ixodes tick species acting as vectors. In the U.S., I. scapularis transmits multiple pathogens, while in Eurasia, I. ricinus and I. persulcatus are responsible for transmission, including Lyme disease and other viruses.
keyPoints: I. ricinus is the vector across Eurasia; I. persulcatus in eastern Russia, China, and Japan.
In the U.S., I. scapularis transmits multiple pathogens beyond B. burgdorferi, including Babesia microti and Powassan virus.
Lyme disease reported frequencies are highest in central Europe and Scandinavia.
Ticks transmit diseases through blood meals at larval, nymphal, and adult stages.

items.title: Mechanism of Lyme Disease Transmission by Ticks
items.content: Borrelia burgdorferi requires at least a 24-hour attachment period in ticks for transmission to the mammalian host. The spirochete adapts its surface proteins, such as OspA and OspC, during different stages of its lifecycle within the tick.
keyPoints: B. burgdorferi must adapt to environments in both ticks and mammals.
OspA is expressed in the midgut; OspC binds Salp15 for mammalian infection.
Transmission requires a minimum 24-hour attachment of the tick.

items.title: Antigenic Variation and Immune Evasion by B. burgdorferi
items.content: B. burgdorferi downregulates OspC after entering the human host, upregulating VlsE for antigenic variation to evade immune detection. This adaptation is crucial for its survival and dissemination.
keyPoints: VlsE undergoes extensive antigenic variation post-infection.
Spirochete uses surface proteins to attach to mammalian components like integrins, glycosaminoglycans, or glycoproteins.
Binding to host molecules (e.g., fibrinogen receptor on platelets) aids dissemination.

items.title: Host Immune Response and Persistence of B. burgdorferi
items.content: The immune system mounts both innate and adaptive responses to control B. burgdorferi, involving cytokines like interleukin 6 and IFN-γ. Despite this, spirochetes can persist in localized niches without antibiotics for years.
keyPoints: Innate response includes complement-mediated lysis; adaptive response involves antibody production.
High levels of proinflammatory cytokines are linked to a Toll-like receptor 1 polymorphism (1805GG).
Spirochetes may persist in niches like joints or nervous system without antibiotic treatment.

items.title: Recognizing Early Lyme Disease: EM Lesions
items.content: Early localized infection in Lyme disease often presents with erythema migrans (EM), a characteristic skin lesion that begins as a red macule or papule at the tick bite site. It expands to form an annular lesion, sometimes with central clearing and peripheral bright red borders.
keyPoints: Incubation period: 3–32 days
Common sites for EM: thigh, groin, axilla
Approximately 20% of patients do not exhibit EM
Lesion characteristics: annular lesion with possible central clearing and peripheral red border

items.title: Identifying Disseminated Lyme Disease in Early Infection
items.content: In disseminated early infection, B. burgdorferi spreads hematogenously, leading to secondary skin lesions and systemic symptoms such as severe headache, neck stiffness, fever, chills, migratory musculoskeletal pain, arthralgias, and fatigue.
keyPoints: ~15% of patients present with nonspecific systemic symptoms
Symptoms: intermittent and changing, often resolve within weeks
Systemic manifestations can include headache, neck stiffness, fever, chills, migratory musculoskeletal pain, arthralgias, fatigue

items.title: Neurologic and Cardiac Involvement in Early Lyme Disease
items.content: Early neurologic symptoms may include meningeal irritation without CSF pleocytosis, while frank neurologic abnormalities such as meningitis or facial palsy develop in ~15% of untreated patients. Cardiac involvement occurs in ~8%, often presenting as atrioventricular block.
keyPoints: Neurologic symptoms: may include meningeal irritation early on
~15% develop frank neurologic abnormalities like meningitis, facial palsy
Cardiac involvement: ~8%, typically atrioventricular block
CSF findings in neurologic Lyme disease: lymphocytic pleocytosis (~100 cells/μL), elevated protein levels

items.title: Characteristics of Lyme Arthritis and Postinfectious Complications
items.content: Lyme arthritis is characterized by joint fluid white cell counts ranging from 500 to 110,000/µL, with most cells being polymorphonuclear leukocytes. Antibiotic therapy usually resolves the condition, but a subset of patients in the northeastern U.S. experience persistent postinfectious Lyme arthritis due to an excessive proinflammatory immune response rather than ongoing infection.
keyPoints: Joint fluid white cell counts range from 500 to 110,000/µL (average, 25,000/µL).
Most cells in joint fluid are polymorphonuclear leukocytes.
Persistent postinfectious Lyme arthritis occurs despite negative culture and PCR for B. burgdorferi after antibiotic therapy.
Excessive proinflammatory immune response characterized by high IFN-γ levels is a key feature of persistent arthritis.

items.title: Pathogenesis and Risk Factors for Postinfectious Lyme Arthritis
items.content: Postinfectious Lyme arthritis involves an excessive proinflammatory immune response with high IFN-γ levels. Risk factors include specific HLA-DRBI*0401 presentation of B. burgdorferi OspA epitopes, a Toll-like receptor 1 polymorphism (1805GG), and an imbalance in CD4+ T effector/regulatory cell ratios.
keyPoints: High IFN-γ levels persist postinfection.
Risk factors include HLA-DRBI*0401 presentation of B. burgdorferi OspA (OspA^164-175).
Toll-like receptor 1 polymorphism 1805GG in patients infected with OspC type A strains.
Imbalance of CD4+ T effector/regulatory cell ratio contributes to disease persistence.

items.title: Chronic Neurologic and Dermatologic Manifestations of Lyme Borreliosis
items.content: Chronic neurologic involvement in Lyme borreliosis can manifest as subtle encephalopathy or axonal polyneuropathy. Acrodermatitis chronica atrophicans, primarily associated with B. afzelii, presents with skin lesions that evolve from reddish-violaceous discoloration to sclerotic or atrophic changes.
keyPoints: Chronic neurologic involvement includes subtle encephalopathy and axonal polyneuropathy.
Acrodermatitis chronica atrophicans is associated with B. afzelii, presenting as skin lesions on acral surfaces.
Neurological manifestations include memory impairment, mood changes, sleep disturbances, and spinal radicular pain.

items.title: Posttreatment Lyme Disease Syndrome (PTLDS) Characteristics
items.content: Despite treatment, approximately 10% of patients with Lyme disease experience persistent symptoms such as pain, neurocognitive issues, or fatigue. These symptoms can persist for months to years and may resemble chronic fatigue syndrome or fibromyalgia.
keyPoints: Approximately 10% of treated Lyme disease patients develop PTLDS.

Symptoms include marked fatigue, severe headache, diffuse musculoskeletal pain, multiple symmetric tender points, joint pain and stiffness, paresthesias, concentration difficulties, and sleep disturbances.
Patients lack evidence of joint inflammation or abnormal neurologic test results.
Anxiety and depression may be present in these patients.
Symptoms are more generalized compared to active Lyme disease.

items.title: Management of Posttreatment Lyme Disease Syndrome
items.content: There is no evidence that persistent symptoms after antibiotic treatment for Lyme disease are due to ongoing infection. Repeated courses of antibiotics do not improve PTLDS and are not recommended.
keyPoints: Persistent subjective symptoms post-antibiotic therapy are not caused by active infection.
Randomized controlled trials show no benefit from repeated antibiotic courses in treating PTLDS.
Repeating antibiotic treatment for PTLDS is not advised.

items.title: PCR vs. Culture for B. burgdorferi Detection
items.content: PCR is superior to culture for detecting B. burgdorferi DNA in joint fluid, especially later in infection when cultures are less effective.
keyPoints: Positive cultures of B. burgdorferi are typically obtained early in the illness.
PCR is more effective than culture for late-stage detection in joint fluid.
Detection of spirochetal DNA in joint fluid does not indicate active infection post-antibiotic treatment.

items.title: Serologic Testing and Its Limitations
items.content: Lyme disease diagnosis relies on clinical presentation and serology, but serologic tests cannot distinguish between active and inactive infections.
keyPoints: Serologic testing may yield negative results in the first several weeks of infection.
A two-test approach (ELISA followed by Western blot) is used for confirmation.
Patients with past Lyme disease often remain seropositive, complicating diagnosis.

items.title: CDC Criteria for Serologic Testing
items.content: The CDC recommends a two-step serologic testing approach and specific criteria for interpreting IgM and IgG Western blots.
keyPoints: Two-step approach: ELISA followed by Western blot if results are equivocal or positive.
IgM Western blot is positive with 2 of 3 bands (23, 39, 41 kDa).
IgG Western blot is positive with 5 of 10 bands (18, 23, 28, 30, 39, 41, 45, 58, 66, 93 kDa).

items.title: Utilizing Modified Two-Test Approach for Early Lyme Borreliosis Diagnosis
items.content: The modified two-test approach, approved by the FDA, uses two EIAs to diagnose Lyme disease early. This method employs a whole-B. burgdorferi sonicate ELISA followed by a VlsE C6 peptide IgG ELISA, providing increased sensitivity during initial weeks without losing specificity.
keyPoints: FDA-approved modified two-test approach
Employs whole-B. burgdorferi sonicate ELISA and VlsE C6 peptide IgG ELISA
Increases sensitivity in the first several weeks of infection
Maintains specificity

items.title: Interpreting Antibody Responses Post Lyme Borreliosis Treatment
items.content: After successful antibiotic treatment for Lyme disease, antibody titers decline slowly. Both IgG and IgM responses may persist for years post-treatment, making a positive IgM response insufficient to confirm recent infection or reinfection without appropriate clinical context.
keyPoints: Antibody titers decline slowly after treatment
IgG and IgM responses can persist for years
Positive IgM alone does not confirm recent infection or reinfection

items.title: Distinguishing Lyme Disease from Other Tick-Borne Illnesses and Conditions
items.content: When diagnosing Lyme disease, it is crucial to differentiate between Borrelia burgdorferi infection and other conditions that may present with similar symptoms or skin manifestations. This includes distinguishing erythema migrans (EM) from cellulitis, allergic reactions, and erythema multiforme, as well as recognizing the potential for co-infections with other tick-borne pathogens.
keyPoints: Classic EM is a slowly expanding erythema with partial central clearing; rapid expansion may suggest cellulitis or an allergic reaction.
Secondary annular lesions in Lyme disease do not involve blistering mucosal lesions, palms, or soles, unlike erythema multiforme.
Co-infections with B. microti (babesiosis), A. phagocytophilum (anaplasmosis), B. miyamotoi, and others can occur, presenting as influenza-like syndrome with fever, myalgias, and cytopenia.
4% of patients with EM may have evidence of co-infection; PCR is the diagnostic test of choice for detecting A. phagocytophilum, B. microti, or B. miyamotoi.

items.title: Identifying Facial Palsy in Lyme Disease
items.content: Facial palsy can be a manifestation of early disseminated Lyme disease and may occur with or without erythema migrans. It is important to consider Lyme disease as a potential cause when facial palsy presents, especially if associated with EM, and to differentiate it from other causes such as herpes simplex virus type 1 (Bell's palsy) and varicella-zoster virus (Ramsay Hunt syndrome).
keyPoints: Facial palsy in Lyme disease often occurs in the early disseminated phase, typically in July, August, or September.
Both IgM and IgG responses to B. burgdorferi are usually positive in cases of facial palsy without EM.

items.title: Avoiding Misdiagnosis of Late-Stage Lyme Disease
items.content: In late-stage Lyme disease, it is important to avoid misdiagnosing chronic fatigue syndrome or fibromyalgia as Lyme disease. Clinicians should be aware that a small percentage of patients with Lyme disease may develop these syndromes, but the term 'chronic Lyme disease' is often used inappropriately for conditions without evidence of ongoing B. burgdorferi infection.
keyPoints: Chronic fatigue syndrome or fibromyalgia should not be mistaken for late-stage Lyme disease.
There is no benefit to repeated courses of antibiotic treatment for post-treatment Lyme disease syndrome (PTLDS), and there are risks associated with such treatment.

items.title: Oral Antibiotics for Early Lyme Disease
items.content: Orally administered antibiotics are generally effective for early Lyme disease, with doxycycline being the preferred choice for men, nonpregnant women, and children over 8 years old. It is also effective against other tick-borne pathogens like A. phagocytophilum.
keyPoints: Doxycycline is first-line treatment for early Lyme disease in eligible patients.
>90% satisfactory outcomes with oral regimens for early Lyme disease.
Doxycycline also covers A. phagocytophilum, B. miyamotoi, and B. mayonii.

items.title: IV Antibiotics for Severe Lyme Disease Manifestations
items.content: Severe manifestations of Lyme disease such as significant neurologic abnormalities or third-degree atrioventricular heart block require IV antibiotic treatment, typically with ceftriaxone.
keyPoints: IV antibiotics are necessary for severe neurologic abnormalities and third-degree AV block.
Ceftriaxone is commonly used for 14–28 days in these cases.
Oral doxycycline may be used for less severe neurologic issues like facial palsy.

items.title: Management of Lyme Arthritis
items.content: Lyme arthritis is initially treated with 30 days of oral antibiotics. If there's an incomplete response, a second course may be given; otherwise, IV ceftriaxone for 28 days is recommended.
keyPoints: Initial treatment for Lyme arthritis: 30-day oral doxycycline or amoxicillin.
Second 30-day oral antibiotic course if initial response is incomplete.
IV ceftriaxone for 28 days if minimal/no response to oral antibiotics.

items.subtopic: CHRONIC LYME DISEASE | PROPHYLAXIS AFTER A TICK BITE | PROGNOSIS | REINFECTION | ☐ PREVENTION
items.title: Management of Posttreatment Lyme Disease Syndrome (PTLDS)
items.content: Posttreatment Lyme disease syndrome (PTLDS) involves persistent symptoms such as musculoskeletal pain, neurocognitive difficulties, or fatigue after appropriate treatment for Lyme disease. It is similar to chronic fatigue syndrome or fibromyalgia and does not benefit from prolonged antibiotic therapy.
keyPoints: PTLDS occurs in a small percentage of patients post-treatment.
Symptoms include musculoskeletal pain, neurocognitive difficulties, and fatigue.
Five double-blind, placebo-controlled trials showed no benefit of further antibiotics.
Treatment should focus on symptom relief rather than prolonged antibiotic courses.
items.reference: Lyme Borreliosis · CHRONIC LYME DISEASE | PROPHYLAXIS AFTER A TICK BITE | PROGNOSIS | REINFECTION | ☐ PREVENTION

items.subtopic: CHRONIC LYME DISEASE | PROPHYLAXIS AFTER A TICK BITE | PROGNOSIS | REINFECTION | ☐ PREVENTION
items.title: Prophylaxis After a Tick Bite and Reinfection Risk
items.content: Antibiotic prophylaxis after a tick bite is generally not recommended due to low infection risk. However, a single 200-mg dose of doxycycline may be given within 72 hours if an engorged I. scapularis nymph is found or follow-up is difficult. Reinfection can occur post-erythema migrans (EM) treatment but not in patients with developed immune responses like Lyme arthritis.
keyPoints: Routine antibiotic prophylaxis after a tick bite is not indicated.
A single 200-mg dose of doxycycline may be used within 72 hours for specific cases.
Reinfection can occur post-EM treatment without adequate immune response.
Patients with Lyme arthritis develop protective immunity and rarely reinfect.
items.reference: Lyme Borreliosis · CHRONIC LYME DISEASE | PROPHYLAXIS AFTER A TICK BITE | PROGNOSIS | REINFECTION | ☐ PREVENTION

items.question: Which organism is primarily responsible for causing Lyme borreliosis?
items.explanation: Lyme borreliosis is caused by Borrelia burgdorferi sensu lato, a group of spirochetes.
items.reference: Lyme Borreliosis · Lyme Borreliosis | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.question: What is the primary vector for transmitting Lyme disease?
items.explanation: The infection is transmitted by ticks of the Ixodes ricinus complex.
items.reference: Lyme Borreliosis · Lyme Borreliosis | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.question: Which genospecies of B. burgdorferi is the major cause of Lyme disease in the United States?
items.explanation: B. burgdorferi sensu stricto is the major cause of Lyme disease in the United States.
items.reference: Lyme Borreliosis · Lyme Borreliosis | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.question: Which typing scheme for B. burgdorferi strains indicates a large pangenome and high pathogenicity?
items.explanation: WGS type A, which includes OspC type A (RST1) strains, has the largest pangenome and is particularly virulent.
items.reference: Lyme Borreliosis · Lyme Borreliosis | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.question: Which tick species is the principal vector for Lyme borreliosis in the eastern United States?
items.explanation: I. scapularis is the principal vector for Lyme borreliosis in the eastern United States.

items.question: Which animal is not involved in the life cycle of B. burgdorferi but is critical to the survival of I. scapularis?
items.explanation: White-tailed deer are not involved in the life cycle of B. burgdorferi but are critical to the survival of I. scapularis.

items.question: What is primarily responsible for the transmission of Lyme disease to humans?
items.explanation: The tiny nymphal tick is primarily responsible for the transmission of Lyme disease to humans.

items.question: Which protein is upregulated in B. burgdorferi as it travels to the tick's salivary gland?
items.explanation: OspC is upregulated in B. burgdorferi as it travels to the tick's salivary gland.

items.question: What is required for B. burgdorferi infection of the mammalian host?
items.explanation: OspC binds a tick salivary-gland protein (Salp15), which is required for infection of the mammalian host.

items.question: Which immune response component is involved in the opsonization of B. burgdorferi?
items.explanation: The adaptive immune response produces specific antibodies that opsonize B. burgdorferi.

items.question: What facilitates the dissemination of B. burgdorferi in the blood?
options: Binding to fibrinogen receptor on activated platelets
items.explanation: Dissemination in the blood is facilitated by binding to the fibrinogen receptor (α1mβ3) on activated platelets.

items.question: Which is the most common early skin manifestation of Lyme disease?
items.explanation: Erythema migrans (EM) is the characteristic early skin manifestation of Lyme disease.

items.question: What percentage of patients do not exhibit erythema migrans during early infection?
items.explanation: Approximately 20% of patients do not exhibit the characteristic skin manifestation, erythema migrans.

items.question: What is a common early neurological symptom in Lyme disease when EM is present?
items.explanation: Symptoms suggestive of meningeal irritation may develop early in Lyme disease when EM is present but are usually not associated with cerebrospinal fluid pleocytosis or an objective neurologic deficit.

items.question: Which cardiac abnormality is most commonly seen during early Lyme disease?
items.explanation: The most common cardiac abnormality is a fluctuating degree of atrioventricular block, which includes first-degree, Wenckebach (second-degree), or complete heart block.

items.question: Which cell type predominantly constitutes the white cell count in joint fluid affected by Lyme arthritis?
items.explanation: Most of the cells in joint fluid during Lyme arthritis are polymorphonuclear leukocytes.

items.question: What is a characteristic feature of postinfectious Lyme arthritis?
options: Negative culture and PCR results for B. burgdorferi in synovial tissue
items.explanation: Postinfectious Lyme arthritis is characterized by negative culture and PCR results for B. burgdorferi.

items.question: Which autoantigens are associated with vascular damage in postinfectious Lyme arthritis?
options: Endothelial cell growth factor, apolipoprotein B-100
items.explanation: Autoantigens associated with vascular damage include endothelial cell growth factor and apolipoprotein B-100.

items.question: Which Borrelia species is primarily associated with acrodermatitis chronica atrophicans in Europe?
items.explanation: Acrodermatitis chronica atrophicans is primarily associated with B. afzelii in Europe.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic symptom of Posttreatment Lyme Disease Syndrome (PTLDS)?
items.explanation: Patients with PTLDS lack evidence of joint inflammation.

items.question: What is the recommended course of action for patients experiencing symptoms of Posttreatment Lyme Disease Syndrome (PTLDS) after completing antibiotic therapy?
items.explanation: Randomized controlled trials have shown that repeated courses of antibiotics do not improve PTLDS symptoms and are not recommended.

items.question: Which condition is NOT mentioned as having similar post-infection sequelae to Posttreatment Lyme Disease Syndrome (PTLDS)?
items.explanation: Encephalopathy is a late manifestation of Lyme disease, not mentioned as similar to PTLDS.

items.question: Which method is considered superior to culture for detecting B. burgdorferi DNA in joint fluid during later stages of Lyme disease?
items.explanation: PCR is greatly superior to culture for the detection of B. burgdorferi DNA in joint fluid later in infection.

items.question: What is a limitation of serologic tests for Lyme disease?
options: They are always negative during early infection
They can distinguish between active and inactive infections
They do not clearly distinguish between active and inactive infections
items.explanation: Serologic tests do not clearly distinguish between active and inactive infection.

items.question: According to the CDC, what is required for an IgM Western blot to be considered positive?
options: Presence of two bands: 39 and 41 kDa
Presence of three bands: 18, 28, and 45 kDa
Presence of two bands out of three: 23, 39, and 41 kDa
items.explanation: An IgM Western blot is positive if two of the following three bands are present: 23, 39, and 41 kDa.

items.question: For which patients does the American College of Physicians recommend serologic testing for Lyme disease?
options: Patients with pain or fatigue syndromes
All patients presenting with joint pain
Patients with at least an intermediate pretest probability of Lyme disease
items.explanation: Serologic testing is recommended for patients with at least an intermediate pretest probability of Lyme disease.

items.question: Which two tests are used in the modified two-test approach for Lyme disease diagnosis?
options: Whole-B. burgdorferi sonicate ELISA and VlsE C6 peptide IgG ELISA
Western blot and whole-B. burgdorferi sonicate ELISA
VlsE C6 peptide IgM ELISA and Western blot
Line immunoblots and multiplexed antibody platforms
items.explanation: The modified two-test approach uses a whole-B. burgdorferi sonicate ELISA followed by a VlsE C6 peptide IgG ELISA.

items.question: What is the advantage of using the modified two-test approach during the first several weeks of Lyme disease infection?
options: It increases specificity without affecting sensitivity.
It eliminates the need for any follow-up tests.
It increases sensitivity without compromising specificity.
It provides quantitative data about antibody responses.
items.explanation: The modified two-test approach increases sensitivity during the first several weeks of infection without compromising specificity.

items.question: Why can a positive IgM response not be interpreted as confirmation of recent Lyme disease infection?
options: IgM responses are always false positives.
IgM responses may persist for years after therapy.
IgM is not produced in response to B. burgdorferi.
The clinical picture must indicate a new infection.
items.explanation: A positive IgM response cannot be interpreted as confirmation of recent infection because the IgM response may persist for years after therapy.

items.question: Which condition may present with erythema multiforme-like lesions but does not involve blistering mucosal lesions or involvement of the palms or soles?
items.explanation: Neither the development of blistering mucosal lesions nor the involvement of the palms or soles is a feature of B. burgdorferi infection.

items.question: What diagnostic test is considered the choice for detecting co-infection with A. phagocytophilum, B. microti, or B. miyamotoi?
items.explanation: PCR of blood is the diagnostic test of choice for co-infection with A. phagocytophilum, B. microti, or B. miyamotoi.

items.question: Which tick-borne agent may cause leukopenia or thrombocytopenia and is often confused with B. burgdorferi due to IgM serologic responses?
items.explanation: A. phagocytophilum may elicit a positive IgM response to B. burgdorferi.

items.question: What is the most common infectious agent causing facial palsy that should be considered in differential diagnosis when Lyme disease is suspected?
items.explanation: The most common infectious agents that cause facial palsy are herpes simplex virus type 1 (Bell's palsy) and varicella-zoster virus.

items.question: Which oral antibiotic is recommended as first-line therapy for early Lyme disease in men, nonpregnant women, and children older than age 8?
items.explanation: Doxycycline is effective for early Lyme disease in these groups and also covers other tick-borne infections.

items.question: For patients with severe objective neurologic abnormalities due to Lyme disease, which treatment is generally recommended?
items.explanation: IV antibiotics like ceftriaxone are used for severe objective neurologic abnormalities.

items.question: What is the recommended treatment duration with oral doxycycline or amoxicillin for Lyme arthritis without concomitant neurologic involvement?
items.explanation: A 30-day course of oral antibiotics is recommended for initial treatment of Lyme arthritis.

items.question: Which antibiotic regimen may be used for patients with persistent joint inflammation after both oral and IV antibiotic treatments?
items.explanation: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory agents, DMARDs, or synovectomy may be successful after persistent inflammation.

items.subtopic: CHRONIC LYME DISEASE | PROPHYLAXIS AFTER A TICK BITE | PROGNOSIS | REINFECTION | ☐ PREVENTION
items.question: Which treatment has been shown to be ineffective in patients with posttreatment Lyme disease syndrome (PTLDS)?
options: IV ceftriaxone followed by oral doxycycline
items.explanation: Five double-blind, placebo-controlled trials have failed to show benefit of further antibiotic therapy in patients with PTLDS.
items.reference: Lyme Borreliosis · CHRONIC LYME DISEASE | PROPHYLAXIS AFTER A TICK BITE | PROGNOSIS | REINFECTION | ☐ PREVENTION

items.subtopic: CHRONIC LYME DISEASE | PROPHYLAXIS AFTER A TICK BITE | PROGNOSIS | REINFECTION | ☐ PREVENTION
items.question: When is doxycycline prophylaxis considered after a tick bite?
options: If an engorged I. scapularis nymph is found or follow-up is difficult
Only if the patient develops symptoms of Lyme disease
After every tick removal regardless of type
items.explanation: Doxycycline prophylaxis may be administered if an attached, engorged I. scapularis nymph is found or follow-up is anticipated to be difficult.
items.reference: Lyme Borreliosis · CHRONIC LYME DISEASE | PROPHYLAXIS AFTER A TICK BITE | PROGNOSIS | REINFECTION | ☐ PREVENTION

items.subtopic: CHRONIC LYME DISEASE | PROPHYLAXIS AFTER A TICK BITE | PROGNOSIS | REINFECTION | ☐ PREVENTION
items.question: What is the general prognosis for patients treated early in Lyme borreliosis?
options: Most patients experience prolonged convalescence with significant deficits
Early treatment usually results in a longer period of recovery
Patients often have minimal or no residual deficits after recovery
The majority do not recover and remain symptomatic
items.explanation: The response to treatment is best early in the disease, with most patients recovering with minimal or no residual deficits.
items.reference: Lyme Borreliosis · CHRONIC LYME DISEASE | PROPHYLAXIS AFTER A TICK BITE | PROGNOSIS | REINFECTION | ☐ PREVENTION

items.statement: B. burgdorferi has a large genome with many biosynthetic proteins.
items.explanation: B. burgdorferi has a small genome and few proteins with biosynthetic activity.
items.reference: Lyme Borreliosis · Lyme Borreliosis | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.statement: Lyme disease was first recognized in Europe.
items.explanation: Lyme disease was first recognized as a separate entity in 1976 in Lyme, Connecticut.
items.reference: Lyme Borreliosis · Lyme Borreliosis | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.statement: I. ricinus is a vector for Lyme borreliosis in both Eurasia and North America.
items.explanation: I. ricinus is the vector in Eurasia, while I. scapularis and I. pacificus are vectors in North America.

items.statement: The number of reported Lyme disease cases annually in the United States exceeds 30,000.
items.explanation: More than 30,000 new cases are reported each summer, with an estimated total of 476,000 annually.

items.statement: Infection of humans with B. burgdorferi is a dead-end event for the spirochete.
items.explanation: Human infection does not allow B. burgdorferi to continue its enzootic cycle.

items.statement: B. burgdorferi can survive indefinitely in the human body without antibiotic treatment.
items.explanation: Without antibiotic therapy, spirochetes may survive in localized niches for several years but not indefinitely.

items.statement: Approximately 15% of patients with Lyme disease present with nonspecific systemic symptoms without erythema migrans.
items.explanation: In about 15% of patients, the infection presents with these nonspecific systemic symptoms.

items.statement: Early neurologic abnormalities in Lyme disease are usually permanent.
items.explanation: These early neurologic abnormalities usually resolve completely within months, though chronic neurologic disease may occur rarely.

items.statement: Persistent infection with B. burgdorferi is the cause of postinfectious Lyme arthritis.
items.explanation: Postinfectious Lyme arthritis results from an excessive proinflammatory immune response, not persistent infection.

items.statement: B. garinii is more likely to cause neuroborreliosis than B. burgdorferi.
items.explanation: B. garinii is especially neurotropic and more likely to cause typical neuroborreliosis.

items.statement: Patients with Posttreatment Lyme Disease Syndrome often exhibit anxiety and depression.
items.explanation: PTLDS patients may exhibit anxiety and depression.

items.statement: Persistent subjective symptoms after antibiotic therapy in Lyme disease are caused by active infection.
items.explanation: There is no evidence that persistent symptoms are due to active infection.

items.statement: PCR can be reliably used to determine the adequacy of antibiotic therapy in Lyme disease.
items.explanation: Detection of spirochetal DNA after antibiotic treatment does not accurately reflect active infection.

items.statement: A positive IgM test result alone is reliable for diagnosing Lyme disease in patients with illness duration greater than two months.
items.explanation: In illnesses of >2 months' duration, a positive IgM test result alone is likely to be false-positive.

items.statement: The modified two-test approach includes a Western blot.
items.explanation: The modified two-test approach dispenses with the Western blot, using two EIAs instead.

items.statement: Antibody titers decline rapidly after successful antibiotic treatment for Lyme disease.
items.explanation: After successful antibiotic treatment, antibody titers decline slowly.

items.statement: A negative serologic test for B. burgdorferi excludes acute Lyme disease.
items.explanation: The diagnosis of early Lyme disease is a clinical one, and clinicians should recognize the limited value of serologic testing in evaluating patients with early Lyme disease.

items.statement: Chronic Lyme disease equates to chronic B. burgdorferi infection.
items.explanation: The term chronic Lyme disease is a misnomer when there is little or no evidence of B. burgdorferi infection.

items.statement: First-generation cephalosporins like cephalexin are effective for treating Lyme disease.
items.explanation: First-generation cephalosporins, such as cephalexin, are not effective against Lyme disease.

items.statement: Maternal-fetal transmission of B. burgdorferi is common and requires special treatment during pregnancy.
items.explanation: Maternal-fetal transmission seems to occur rarely, if at all; standard therapy for the illness is recommended.

items.subtopic: CHRONIC LYME DISEASE | PROPHYLAXIS AFTER A TICK BITE | PROGNOSIS | REINFECTION | ☐ PREVENTION
items.statement: A vaccine for Lyme disease is currently available commercially.
items.explanation: No vaccine is currently available commercially for the prevention of Lyme disease.
items.reference: Lyme Borreliosis · CHRONIC LYME DISEASE | PROPHYLAXIS AFTER A TICK BITE | PROGNOSIS | REINFECTION | ☐ PREVENTION

items.subtopic: CHRONIC LYME DISEASE | PROPHYLAXIS AFTER A TICK BITE | PROGNOSIS | REINFECTION | ☐ PREVENTION
items.statement: Reinfection with Lyme disease is common in patients who have developed an expanded immune response.
items.explanation: Patients with an expanded immune response to the spirochete rarely, if ever, acquire the infection again.
items.reference: Lyme Borreliosis · CHRONIC LYME DISEASE | PROPHYLAXIS AFTER A TICK BITE | PROGNOSIS | REINFECTION | ☐ PREVENTION

items.assertion: B. burgdorferi depends on its host for most of its nutritional requirements.
items.reason: The spirochete has few proteins with biosynthetic activity.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Lyme Borreliosis · Lyme Borreliosis | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.assertion: All four genospecies of B. burgdorferi sensu lato cause Lyme disease in Asia.
items.reason: B. garinii, B. afzelii, and B. bavariensis are the major causes in Asia.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but not all four genospecies cause Lyme disease in Asia.
items.reference: Lyme Borreliosis · Lyme Borreliosis | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.assertion: B. burgdorferi binds to native type 1 collagen lattices.
items.reason: This binding may explain why the organism is commonly aligned with collagen fibrils in the extracellular matrix.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Patients with a Toll-like receptor 1 polymorphism have lower levels of proinflammatory cytokines.
items.reason: This polymorphism is associated with exceptionally high levels of proinflammatory cytokines when infected with B. burgdorferi RST1 strains.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the reason is true, as the polymorphism leads to higher cytokine levels.

items.assertion: Migratory musculoskeletal pain is a common symptom during the early stages of Lyme disease.
items.reason: The typical pattern consists of migratory pain in joints, tendons, bursae, muscles, or bones without joint swelling.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: In untreated Lyme disease patients in Europe, meningopolyneuritis is typically absent.
items.reason: The first neurologic sign in Europe is characteristically radicular pain followed by CSF pleocytosis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; meningopolyneuritis (Bannwarth's syndrome) can occur, while the reason is true.

items.assertion: Postinfectious Lyme arthritis involves a dysregulated proinflammatory immune response.
items.reason: This is characterized by exceptionally high IFN-γ levels that persist after infection.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Acrodermatitis chronica atrophicans is primarily associated with B. burgdorferi in Europe.
items.reason: This condition is more commonly linked to B. afzelii infection.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because acrodermatitis chronica atrophicans is associated with B. afzelii, not B. burgdorferi.

items.assertion: Serologic tests for Lyme disease should not be used as screening procedures in patients with pain or fatigue syndromes.
items.reason: The probability of a false-positive serologic result is higher than that of a true-positive result in these patients.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: The sensitivity of PCR determinations in CSF from patients with neuroborreliosis is high.
items.reason: PCR is superior to culture for detecting B. burgdorferi DNA in joint fluid.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the sensitivity of PCR in CSF is much lower than that in joint fluid.

items.assertion: The modified two-test approach is approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration.
items.reason: This approach uses a whole-B. burgdorferi sonicate ELISA followed by a VlsE C6 peptide IgG ELISA.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the modified two-test approach is approved.

items.assertion: Lyme arthritis usually presents with strong IgG antibody responses.
items.reason: Patients with Lyme arthritis have the strongest IgG antibody responses seen in Lyme borreliosis, with reactivity to many spirochetal proteins.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: STARI is caused by B. burgdorferi.
items.reason: The cause of STARI has not yet been identified.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the cause of STARI has not been identified, while the reason is true.

items.assertion: Oral doxycycline is used by many clinicians in the United States for less severe neurologic abnormalities.
items.reason: Systematic trials have not been conducted, but it has shown effectiveness similar to IV antibiotics in Europe.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not directly explain why oral doxycycline is used.

items.assertion: Long-term persistence of B. burgdorferi has been documented in large series after treatment.
items.reason: There are a few case reports of persistent infection, but no documentation in large series.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; long-term persistence has not been documented in large series.
